name: Maine
delegatedLocalAuthorities: Maine's plain-language homeowner guidance gives enough clarity to keep the launch wedge transaction-first and evidence-first.
buyerSellerSummary: Maine supports buyer-risk and owner-risk framing when tank status is uncertain and records are incomplete.
recordLookupSummary: Use any closure paperwork, prior fuel evidence, and property history before assuming the question is resolved.
sweepFirstSummary: A sweep-first route helps keep the answer honest when old tank infrastructure is suspected but not proven.
removalVsAbandonmentSummary: Tank disposition depends on what is confirmed on site and whether contamination concern changes the route.
leakReportingSummary: Leak concern may widen the case from simple tank handling into documentation and cleanup review.
insuranceCleanupSummary: Cleanup direction may matter more than price once the route moves beyond a locate question.
quickAnswer: In Maine, start with evidence and official homeowner guidance before assuming the tank question is settled.
commonTriggers: A buyer needs confidence before closing on an older home.
The seller cannot show clear closure paperwork.
There are clues of prior oil heat but the tank status is uncertain.
cautions: Do not assume the file is clean just because the property is old.
Do not move straight to removal talk without confirming the route.
Do not treat a leak concern as identical to ordinary closure.
strongestTriggerStates: An older home shows prior oil-heat clues, but there is no clean closure paperwork before closing.
The buyer wants confidence on tank status before turning the issue into a price or removal fight.
Plain-language guidance supports verification first, but the record stack is still thin.
firstMoves: Pull homeowner paperwork, prior fuel history, and any closure record before widening the route.
Use a sweep or locate if visible clues are stronger than the file.
If the documents point to contamination or cleanup review, stop treating the route as ordinary closure.
documentTargets: Prior fuel-delivery, conversion, or burner-replacement records showing when the system changed.
Closure paperwork, contractor invoices, and any DEP-related homeowner guidance tied to the property.
Any cleanup, spill, or no-further-action-style document that changes the route.
escalationSignals: Odor, staining, or excavation history suggesting contamination concern.
Contractor or inspection notes that confirm a tank or mention possible release conditions.
Any document trail showing the case already moved into cleanup review.

sourceStack.title: Maine DEP Plain Talk on Heating Oil Tanks
